name: Alex Jones 9/11
description: The 'Alex Jones 9/11' concept refers to the conspiracy theories promoted primarily by American radio host and conspiracy theorist Alex Jones regarding the September 11, 2001 attacks. Jones has, over time, presented various claims, ranging from governmental negligence allowing the attacks to occur, to the more extreme assertion that the attacks were a deliberate inside job orchestrated by elements within the US government, potentially in collaboration with international entities, to justify wars in Afghanistan and Iraq, expand surveillance powers, and consolidate control. He has cited anomalies in the collapse of the World Trade Center towers, alleged foreknowledge by government officials, and the perceived lack of accountability for those responsible as evidence. Mainstream scholarly and scientific perspectives overwhelmingly reject these claims. The official investigations, including the 9/11 Commission Report, attribute the attacks to al-Qaeda. Structural engineers and scientists attribute the collapse of the towers to the impact and subsequent fires weakening the steel structures. Jones' theories have been widely criticized for lacking credible evidence, promoting misinformation, and causing pain to the victims' families. His promotion of these theories has resulted in legal action, including defamation lawsuits filed by families of Sandy Hook shooting victims who were also subjected to Jones' false claims. His rhetoric has been accused of contributing to a climate of distrust and paranoia.
subtitle: Conspiracy theories surrounding September 11 attacks

claims.evidence: Controlled demolition theories about the collapse of the World Trade Center towers; alleged foreknowledge by government officials; inconsistencies in the official narrative.
claims.criticism: The 9/11 Commission Report and other investigations attribute the attacks to al-Qaeda. Structural engineers explain the collapse as a result of fire-induced weakening of the steel structures. Alleged inconsistencies are explained by established facts and eyewitness accounts.

claims.claim: The US government allowed 9/11 to happen.
claims.evidence: Alleged stand-down orders preventing interception of the hijacked planes; claims that intelligence agencies possessed warnings about the attacks but failed to act.
claims.criticism: The 9/11 Commission Report found no evidence of a deliberate stand-down order. Intelligence failures and communication breakdowns are acknowledged, but not as part of a coordinated plot. The speed and unexpected nature of the attacks are cited as contributing factors.

claims.claim: The motive for 9/11 was to justify wars in the Middle East and expand government powers.
claims.evidence: The subsequent invasions of Afghanistan and Iraq; the passage of the Patriot Act and increased surveillance measures.
claims.criticism: The invasions of Afghanistan and Iraq were presented as responses to the 9/11 attacks and the perceived threat of terrorism. The Patriot Act was framed as a necessary tool for national security. Correlation does not equal causation.

keyFigures.name: Alex Jones
keyFigures.role: Radio host and conspiracy theorist promoting 9/11 conspiracy theories

keyFigures.name: David Ray Griffin
keyFigures.role: Author and theologian who promoted controlled demolition theories

keyFigures.name: Philip Zelikow
keyFigures.role: Executive Director of the 9/11 Commission, often criticized by conspiracy theorists

mythologyConnections: The concept of a powerful, hidden enemy manipulating events.
The idea of a scapegoat to justify actions.

timeline.event: Alex Jones begins promoting 9/11 conspiracy theories on his radio show and website.
The 9/11 Commission Report is released, but is questioned by conspiracy theorists.
Alex Jones continues to promote and elaborate on his 9/11 theories.
